coredata.dc:title: Sour whey-derived carbon dots; synthesis, characterization, antioxidant activity and antimicrobial performance on foodborne pathogens

coredata.dc:description: Zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, known as carbon dots (CDs), have become increasingly popular as nanostructures for food preservation due to their antimicrobial and antioxidant properties. In this study, heteroatom-doped CDs were effectively synthesized from sour whey using a hydrothermal method. The size, chemical, and structural composition, as well as the optical and spectral properties of the synthesized CDs, were thoroughly characterized. Furthermore, this study also investigated the antimicrobial, antioxidant, and cytotoxic properties of CDs. Semi-spherical N- and P-doped CDs with size less than 7.8 nm were synthesized. The results of the cytotoxicity test suggested that CDs are low-toxic nanomaterial. The susceptibility of Salmonella Typhimurium was found to be higher than that of Listeria monocytogenes, with a minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) and minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) of 500 μL/mL and 1000 μL/mL, respectively. Based on SEM observations, it was observed that CDs derived from whey mainly targeted the bacterial cell wall. In the modeling of antimicrobial kinetics, the Weibull model provided a strong fit (R2 > 0.9), particularly for the inactivation kinetics of S. Typhimurium. CDs produced from abundant sour whey, which is a sustainable, inexpensive, and eco-friendly source, exhibited important characteristics such as low cytotoxicity and antimicrobial capabilities. The synthesized CDs have potential as antimicrobial or antioxidant agents in food formulations, as well as UV-blockers and nanofillers in the development of composite polymers for food packaging.

head.ce:title: Sour whey-derived carbon dots; synthesis, characterization, antioxidant activity and antimicrobial performance on foodborne pathogens

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, known as carbon dots (CDs), have become increasingly popular as nanostructures for food preservation due to their antimicrobial and antioxidant properties. In this study, heteroatom-doped CDs were effectively synthesized from sour whey using a hydrothermal method. The size, chemical, and structural composition, as well as the optical and spectral properties of the synthesized CDs, were thoroughly characterized. Furthermore, this study also investigated the antimicrobial, antioxidant, and cytotoxic properties of CDs. Semi-spherical N- and P-doped CDs with size less than 7.8 nm were synthesized. The results of the cytotoxicity test suggested that CDs are low-toxic nanomaterial. The susceptibility of Salmonella Typhimurium was found to be higher than that of Listeria monocytogenes, with a minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) and minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) of 500 μL/mL and 1000 μL/mL, respectively. Based on SEM observations, it was observed that CDs derived from whey mainly targeted the bacterial cell wall. In the modeling of antimicrobial kinetics, the Weibull model provided a strong fit (R2 > 0.9), particularly for the inactivation kinetics of S. Typhimurium. CDs produced from abundant sour whey, which is a sustainable, inexpensive, and eco-friendly source, exhibited important characteristics such as low cytotoxicity and antimicrobial capabilities. The synthesized CDs have potential as antimicrobial or antioxidant agents in food formulations, as well as UV-blockers and nanofillers in the development of composite polymers for food packaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are semi-spherical nanomaterials containing carbon, oxygen, and hydrogen with a size of less than 10 nm (Sciortino, Cannizzo, & Messina, 2018). The exceptional properties of CDs, such as their aqueous dispersibility, high photoluminescence, good photo and chemical stability, biocompatibility, low toxicity, versatile surface chemistry, and ease of preparation and use, have made them vital candidates in numerous research projects (Khan, Mohammad, & Yoon, 2022; B. Wang & Lu, 2022). These features are important for the use of CDs in the design of chemical sensors, bioimaging, biotherapy, biopreservation, biodegradation, and photoelectrocatalysis (Ghorbani et al., 2021; Koutsogiannis, Thomou, Stamatis, Gournis, & Rudolf, 2020; X. Wang, Feng, Dong, & Huang, 2019). The synthesis of CDs can be accomplished using different techniques and precursors, which can be tailored based on the desired physicochemical properties such as size, quantity, morphology, surface charge, hydrophobicity, and toxicity (Ge et al., 2021).

CDs can be prepared from chemical carbon precursors and renewable sources (Alamdari, Almasi, Moradi, & Akhgari, 2023). While the use of pure chemicals may lead to highly efficient processes as well as product uniformity, owing to the utilization of significant amounts of energy, difficult reaction conditions, and the generation of environmentally detrimental substances, the process is not economically feasible (Fan, Zhang, Bhandari, & Yang, 2020; Kousheh, Moradi, Tajik, & Molaei, 2020). Consequently, the use of clean, ecological, and non-toxic precursors such as low-cost biomass and agro-industrial waste is a priority for researchers (Ezati, Rhim, Molaei, & Rezaei, 2022; Kuswandi & Moradi, 2019). Over the last few years, the antibacterial properties of CDs have been considered for various foodborne bacterial and fungal strains (Ghorbani, Tajik, Moradi, Molaei, & Alizadeh, 2022; Salimi, Moradi, Tajik, & Molaei, 2021). While a specific mechanism has not yet been identified for this characteristic, the primary antimicrobial mechanism of CDs is commonly attributed to the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), alterations in cell wall permeability, leakage of cytoplasm, and conformational changes in DNA (Q. Li, Shen, & Xing, 2022).

The CDs were prepared from milk (D. Wang et al., 2016), milk proteins (Bajpai, D’Souza, & Suhail, 2019), expired milk (Athika et al., 2019), and yogurt (Moonrinta et al., 2018). Recently, dairy industrial waste has been included in the production of certain bio-nanomaterials that are important for both industry and consumption (Ahmad et al., 2019). Whey, a prevalent yellowish by-product of various cheeses, is produced in over 160 million tons annually worldwide (Szudera-Kończal, Myszka, Kubiak, & Majcher, 2020). Although whey is integrated into the production of some materials at an industrial level, it is lost in most plants and is considered a waste product in the food industry (Chandrapala, 2018; Kasmi, 2018). Whey is obtained either by enzyme coagulation (sweet whey) or from the cottage and lactic cheese production (sour/acid whey) and consists of water, lactose, protein, minerals, lactic acid, and fat (Szudera-Kończal et al., 2020).

Typically, green synthesis of CDs does not require excessive doping of the CDs surface with reagents. This is because the precursors used in green synthesis already contain a variety of compounds such as proteins, sugars, lipids, vitamins, and other unused resources (Del-Toro-Sánchez et al., 2021; Moradi, Kousheh, et al., 2021). This approach not only reduces environmental pollution but also adds value to CDs (Rodríguez-Félix, Graciano-Verdugo et al., 2022). The utilization of renewable biomass and by-products from food resources for the synthesis of CDs has gained significant attention (Bressi, Balu, Iannazzo, & Espro, 2023). To the best of our knowledge, there is no research on the use of whey biomass for synthesis of CDs. Whey liquid contains various compounds, making it a suitable precursor for doped-CDs synthesis; however, it has gained attention owing to the abundance and low cost of whey byproducts, as well as the potential environmental benefits of utilizing waste materials. Our preliminary study showed that CDs synthesized from sweet whey had lower antimicrobial and antioxidant activities. Therefore, we decided to use sour whey for hetero-atom-doped CDs synthesis. Additionally, the antimicrobial, antioxidant, and cytotoxic properties of the resulting CDs were assessed to explore their potential applications in food and biotherapy.

The current study, lactic whey, also known as sour whey, was employed as a precursor for CD. Sour whey was generously provided by the Pegah Company of West Azerbaijan Province (Urmia, Iran). To prepare the CD, 300 mL of untreated lactic whey was poured into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel chamber and heated at 200 °C for 9 h in a furnace. After cooling, the resulting solution was centrifuged at 15000 rpm for 20 min, and the supernatant was collected and filtered through a 0.22 μm syringe filter with a PTFE membrane (Millipore, MA, USA). The solution was then lyophilized and stored at 4 ± 1 °C until further use.

Instrumental characterization of as-prepared CDs was performed using the following instruments: Thermo Scientific NanoDrop spectrophotometer (2000 C, Waltham, USA) for the UV–Vis absorbance spectrum (200-700 nm at 1 nm resolution), JASCO FP2200 fluorescence spectrophotometer (South Korea) for spectrofluorometric scan, Leo 906 E for Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM; Carl Zeiss, Germany), and Scatteroscope particle size analyzer (PSA) (Qudix Inc., South Korea) for particles morphology and size distribution, Thermo Nicolet spectrometers (Nexus® 670, USA) for FTIR (Fourier transform infrared) analysis and chemical features investigation, D5000 Simens X-ray diffractometer (XRD; Siemens AG, Karlsruhe, Germany) to crystalline property study, Microtrac particle analyzers (Nanotrac wave II, Krefeld, Germany) to measure zeta potential and X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Berlin, Germany) to examine the elemental composition. In addition, carbon-13 and hydrogen-1 nuclear magnetic resonance (13C and 1H NMR) experiments were performed using Bruker 400 MHz NMR spectrometer (Bruker, Advance 400, Hamburg, Germany) with resonance frequencies of 250 MHz. The polydispersity of the synthesized CDs was determined using dynamic light scattering (DLS) on a Zetasizer particle size analyzer ZEN 3600 instrument (Malvern, Malvern Instruments Ltd, UK).

This technique is appropriate for screening antimicrobial activity of biomaterials. Listeria monocytogenes PTCC 1298 and Salmonella Typhimurium PTCC 1709, two important foodborne pathogens were obtained from the Iranian Research Organization for Science and Technology (IROST, Tehran, Iran) and were used in all antimicrobial tests. For the assay, each bacterial strain was maintained for 18 h at 37 °C in Luria-Bertani broth (Quelab Laboratories, Quebec, Canada), and the turbidity of the bacterial inoculum was adjusted to ∼ 1.5 × 106 CFU/mL using a spectrophotometer (Novaspec II; Pharmacia LKB, Uppsala, Sweden) at 600 nm (Balouiri, Sadiki, & Ibnsouda, 2016) and spread on Muller Hinton agar (Ibresco Co., Tehran, Iran). Next, wells with a diameter of 8 mm were created using a sterile punch, and filled with CDs at concentrations of 25, 50, 100, and 200 mg/mL (100 μL each). The diameter of the clear zone of inhibition was measured for each bacterium and the concentration of CDs after 24 h of incubation at 37 °C.

The minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) and minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) of CDs against bacterial strains were examined using a broth micro-dilution assay according to the standard method described by the Clinical and Laboratory Standards Institute (CLSI, 2012). The lowest concentration of CDs with the ability to inhibit more than 99% and 99.9% of the bacterial population in each well (i.e., ∼1.5 × 105 CFU/mL) were identified as MIC99 and MBC, respectively.

The semi-quantitative dynamic shake flask (DSF) assay was used to determine the lethal activity of CDs during a specific contact time (Moradi, Kousheh, et al., 2021). The bacterial suspension and CDs were added into a flask containing tryptone soya broth (Quelab Laboratories) to reach a final bacterial population of ∼1.5 × 105 CFU/mL at 1 × MIC concentration of CDs. Then, the flask was kept in an orbital incubator (37 °C, 24 h, 120 rpm; Memmert, Germany), and bacterial counts were determined at 2-h intervals using viable cell counting. The antibacterial effect of CDs during the inactivation process was described by the Log-Linear and Weibull models (Juneja et al., 2020) using MATLAB (version 2018b, The MathWorks, Inc. Natick, MA).

Morphological alterations in bacterial cells in the presence of CDs have been observed using SEM (Saldanha et al., 2020). Bacterial cells were initially treated with CDs at 1 × MIC concentration for 18 h at 37 °C. Untreated (control) and treated bacterial cells were collected by centrifugation (4000 rpm for 10 min) and washed with phosphate buffer solution (PBS; pH = 7). The bacterial cells were then fixed with glutaraldehyde (2.5% at 22 °C), washed with PBS, and dehydrated with graded acetone solutions (50%, 60%, 70%, 80%, 90%, 95%, and 100%) for 30 min each step. Then, cells were dried at ambient temperature, coated with gold, and observed by SEM (JSM-6500F, JEOL Ltd., Akishima, Tokyo, Japan).

Two methods were used to determine the antioxidant performance of CDs. In the ABTS [2,2′-Azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid)] test, a radical ABTS working solution containing potassium persulfate (Merck; Darmstadt, Germany) at 2.45 mM and ABTS (Sigma-Aldrich) at 7 mM were prepared (1:1), mixed in a stoppered flask, allowed to react for 16 h (22 °C) and adjusted to an absorbance of 0.7 ± 0.02 at 734 nm by diluting it with ethanol. Next, the ABTS dilution (2 mL) was mixed vigorously with various CD concentrations (200 μL from 0.5, 1, and 2 mg/mL), and held at ambient temperature for 1 min. Finally, the absorbance was read at 734 nm, and ABTS radical scavenging activity was measured using Eq. (1) (Aliakbarlu, Ghiasi, & Bazargani-Gilani, 2018).
(Eq.1) ABTS radical scavenging activity (%) = (Acontrol – Asample)/ Acontrol × 100
Acontrol and Asample are the absorbances of ABTS working solution without CDs and test solution, respectively. Butylated hydroxytoluene (BHT) was used as a reference antioxidant.

DPPH assay was also used for measuring antioxidant performance (Ghorbani et al., 2022). Briefly, the DPPH solution (1 mL, 4 mg/mL methanol) was added to 1 mL CD concentration (0.1, 0.2, and 0.3 mg/mL) and kept for 1 h at dark and ambient conditions before absorbance determination at 517 nm. The radical scavenging percentage (% RS) was estimated using Eq. (2):
(Eq. 2)

A0 and A1 are the absorptions of DPPH solution and antioxidant solution with DPPH, respectively.

The cytotoxicity on the HCT-116, a human colon cancer cell line, was assessed using the thiazolyl blue tetrazolium bromide (MTT) technique (Varasteh, Braber, Kraneveld, Garssen, & Fink-Gremmels, 2018). HCT-116 (10000 cells per well) cells were grown in standard culture conditions of Dulbeccoʼs modified Eagleʼs medium (DMEM, Sigma-Aldrich) with 10% FBS which was maintained at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere with 5% CO2 in a 96-well plate. After 24 h, when the cells were ready for the experiment, different concentrations of CDs (0.31–10 mg/mL) were utilized for cell treatment and maintained for 48 h. Subsequently, cells were exposed to 20 μL yellow MTT solution (3 mg/mL) for 3 h. The absorbance was assessed at 570 nm by Chromate 4300 Microplate Reader according to the standard protocol. The following equation (Eq. (3)) was employed to calculate cell viability as a percentage of the control group:
(Eq.3) Cell viability (%) = (mean ODtreatment/mean ODcontrol) × 100

Each analysis (except for instrumental characterization) was carried out at least in triplicate. Analysis of variance (ANOVA) was performed for data analysis with Duncan's multiple range test (p < 0.05). Statistical analysis was performed using Graph Pad Prism software version 8.01 (GraphPad Software, USA) and expressed as the average ± standard deviation.

In the study of the optical behavior of CDs, two phenomena are expected to occur: photoluminescence (PL) emission (different colors) ranging from blue to red under UV irradiation (Sciortino et al., 2018) and spectral absorption in the UV region, which is sometimes accompanied with an absorption tail stretching into the visible region (Javed & O'Carroll, 2021). This bright emission behavior of CDs (Fig. 1 A), which is similar to the molecular fluorophores, is either attributed to the fluorophore's nature of surface-bound or adsorbed molecules on CDs or due to the structural electronic transition of the CDs (Hinterberger, Damm, Haines, Guldi, & Peukert, 2019). The UV–Vis spectrum of CDs and the corresponding visual color under UV light is shown in Fig. 1A. Based on previous reports (Javed & O'Carroll, 2021; Salimi, Tajik, Molaei, & Moradi, 2019), the appearance of the observed bright green fluorescence along with a sharp peak (282 nm) is related to the π-π* transition which occurred in the conjugated CC and CN functional groups. Ezati, Rhim, Molaei, and Rezaei (2022) reported the observation of intense green light under UV excitation in all types of S, N, and B doped CDs, attributing it to the quantum confinement effect. A tail extending (quasi-peak) (348 nm) is linked to the n−π* transition of the CO and –NH2 groups. These findings demonstrate a successful CD synthesis. The fluorescence emission results (Fig. 1B) exhibited emission bands at 439 with quasi-peak at around 467 nm (excitation wavelength at 350 nm). Notably, as the excitation wavelength gradually increases (290-400 nm), the emission peak positions shifted to a higher wavelength, and its intensity decreased dramatically after reaching the maximum (λemission: 439 nm). The presence of carboxylic and amide groups, or a single transition mode, could be the cause of this excitation-independent peak (Liu et al., 2020; Russo, Apicella, & Ciajolo, 2019).

As discussed earlier, CDs can be produced from a large variety of organic and inorganic materials. In this study, CDs were synthesized from inexpensive, eco-friendly, and abundant dairy waste (sour whey) and characterized. To illustrate the particle size distribution of the CDs, the PSA test results were presented (Fig. 1C). CDs have an average size of 7.8 nm with a zeta potential of −25 mV. This finding confirms that the degree of electrostatic repulsion between adjacent similar charged CDs will be much higher than the attraction forces and provide the required stability for the dispersed system with no aggregation (Molaei, Farhadi, Forough, & Hajizadeh, 2018). The existence of a negative charge results in electrostatic repulsion between the particles, which prevents them from clumping together and promotes dispersion stability. The PDI was also measured as an indicator of the stability and uniformity of the CDs. A higher PDI value is associated with a wider range of particle sizes, while a lower PDI value indicates more uniform particle sizes. Literature suggests that PDI values closer to 0 represent monodisperse particles, while values closer to 1 indicate polydisperse particles (Rodríguez-Félix et al., 2019). The obtained PDI value of 0.509 indicates that the CDs exhibited a uniform distribution in the suspension, but with some degree of polydispersity. Furthermore, the captured TEM micrograph (Fig. 1D) shows that the CDs are semi-spherical in shape.

According to the chemical shifts (δ) relative to the solvent signal as an internal reference, the obtained 1H NMR spectrum of CDs (Fig. 2 A) can be considered in four different regions. The signals (1-3 ppm) is being linked to sp3 C–H protons (-CH3). In addition, the signal at δ = 7.4 ppm can be identified as an aromatic or sp2 proton, and the relatively strong signals at δ = 9.4 ppm are in concordance with the aldehydic protons (De & Karak, 2013). These findings confirm the presence of different sets of hydrogen atoms bonded to carbon species.

In 13C NMR spectra (Fig. 2B), the hybridized carbon atoms which correspond to aliphatic (sp3) carbon atoms were found in the range of 10–60 ppm. The signals in the range of 60–80 ppm (e.g., signals around 63, 67, and 72 ppm) are attributed to carbon. This demonstrates C-O-C, COOH, and C-O, respectively. In δ = 100-130 ppm, CC aromatic or sp2 carbons can be identified and the signals at around 152, 162, and 178 ppm may be due to the presence of the carboxylic group (aromatic) and aldehyde group, respectively (Arroyave et al., 2021).

The molecular structure and functional groups of the as-synthesized CDs were evaluated by FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 4 A). The characteristic stretching vibrations of N-H/O-H bonds, C–H, CO, and vibrations of C-O-C bonds in the CDs spectrum can be observed at 3394 cm−1, 2925 cm−1, 1668 cm−1, and 1603 cm−1, respectively (Rodríguez-Félix et al., 2021). Moreover, absorption corresponding to the stretching vibrations of C-O and C–N can also be observed at 1407 cm−1 and 1228 cm−1, respectively (Estrella-Osuna et al., 2022; Rodríguez-Félix, Corte-Tarazón et al., 2022). Also, the peaks at 1122 cm−1, 1043 cm−1, 781 cm−1, and 613 cm−1 can be ascribed to the stretching of PO, P-O-C, P-O-H, and the bending mode of P−O, respectively (Kalaiyarasan, Joseph, & Kumar, 2020). By comparing the obtained data with those published by Kumar, Kumari, et al. (2019) and Yang et al. (2018), the synthesis of heteroatoms-rich CDs can be confirmed. It should be noted that other weak absorption bands at 547 and 467 cm−1 can be assigned to the presence of CaO and O-Ca-O groups, respectively (Choudhary, Koppala, & Swamiappan, 2015).

XRD analysis was performed to evaluate the crystalline structure and phases of the synthesized CDs, (Fig. 4). The results revealed a diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 23.00°, which is linked to the diffraction plane (002) indexed for graphitic materials (Lin, Ding, Zhang, & Huang, 2018). However, in agreement with some reports, the weak diffraction peaks at around 2θ = 23.56° are probably due to the presence of CDs doped with P heteroatom (S. Kumar, Kumari, et al., 2019). Moreover, other represented peaks can be attributed to crystalline moieties of some residual mineral salts such as sylvite (KCl) (2θ = 28.06°, 40.24°, and 49.91°) and potassium sodium calcium phosphate (2θ = 31.22°, 31.41°, and 45.12°) (Llamas-Unzueta et al., 2021).

This study investigated the antibacterial activity of CDs in both agar and broth media as well as their impact on the morphological characteristics of two prevalent pathogens, L. monocytogenes, and S. Typhimurium. The results of antimicrobial activity were expressed as the mean diameter of the inhibiting zone around each well (Fig. 5 ). Similar to previous studies (Boobalan et al., 2020; Salimi et al., 2021), the antimicrobial activity of CDs was found to be dependent on their concentration in this study (Fig. 5). At a concentration of 200 mg/mL, comparable antimicrobial activity was observed against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, resulting in a 23 mm inhibitory zone. In accordance with the classification proposed by Ghorbani et al. (2022), both bacterial strains were sensitive and extremely sensitive to CDs at 25 and 200 mg/mL concentration, respectively. Antibacterial activity was not observed when whey solution, which was used as a precursor for the synthesis of CDs, was tested.

Additionally, antimicrobial activity was tested in the broth microdilution method and was expressed as MIC and MBC. The results demonstrated that Gram-negative bacteria were more susceptible to CDs than Gram-positive bacteria in broth medium. S. Typhimurium (Gram-negative strain) was more susceptible with MIC and MBC of 500 μg/mL, than L. monocytogenes (Gram-positive strain) with MIC and MBC of 1000 μg/mL, representing that the cell wall is likely the target of an antimicrobial mechanism of action of prepared CDs. It is crucial to consider various factors such as the specifications of CDs, microbial strain specifications, experimental methods, and culture used when comparing MIC values between different studies. For example, MIC of CDs (8.9 nm) synthesized from glucose against L. monocytogenes was 312 μg/mL and there were no alterations in the MIC values when these CDs were doped with boron (Ezati, Rhim, Molaei, Priyadarshi, et al., 2022). A previous report indicated a low MIC value of 64 μg/mL (Dong et al., 2017) for CDs against L. monocytogenes. However, a higher MIC value of CDs (>1000 μg/mL) on S. Typhimurium has also been reported (Otis et al., 2019). According to the literature, the antimicrobial activity of CDs is linked to many factors of which the doping state of CDs is one of the key factors (Q. Li et al., 2022; Pandiyan et al., 2020). Some studies have found that nitrogen-containing CDs have antibacterial effects on both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria (Chatzimitakos & Stalikas, 2020). Another study (H. Li et al., 2018), CDs prepared by electrochemical treatment of vitamin C not only inactivate a wide broad-spectrum of bacteria, including different Gram-positive and negative strains but also effectively inhibits the growth of Rhizoctonia Solani and Pyricularia Grisea. It should be emphasized that the size of CDs does not solely affect their functional properties, such as antimicrobial, antioxidant, and cytotoxic features. Factors such as CDs shape, surface charge, and doping also play critical roles (Varghese & Balachandran, 2021). Establishing a direct correlation between the size of CDs and their functional properties is challenging. It would be beneficial to synthesize CDs with varying sizes, shapes, and zeta potentials, and subsequently compare their performances to draw conclusive findings. However, when comparing CDs under identical conditions, smaller CDs can more easily enter bacterial cells and exhibit potent antimicrobial performance (Zhao, Liu, Zhang, Xiao, & Li, 2021). Typically, the antibacterial activity of negatively charged CDs is weaker than that of positively charged CDs (Moradi, Kousheh, et al., 2021). These findings may contribute to the moderate antimicrobial activity of the anionic CDs synthesized in this study, which were repelled by a negatively charged microbial cell wall.

To assess the effect of CDs on the cell wall and morphology of bacterial cells, SEM micrographs of microbial cells before and after CDs treatment were captured and are illustrated in Fig. 7 . In untreated samples, the typical structures of L. monocytogenes and S. Typhimurium cells with a smooth appearance are shown. In contrast, in CDs-treated cells, certain morphological changes in the cell membranes and cell integrity were recorded in the CDs at 1 × MIC. A completely disrupted/deformed and rough cell surface was observed in bacterial cells. SEM observation showed a large deformation within S. Typhimurium, representing more cell lysis and rupture. Additionally, in the presence of CDs, cells adhered to each other. Previously, Ezati, Rhim, Molaei, Priyadarshi et al. (2022) reported that treatment of Gram-positive bacteria and Gram-negative bacteria with glucose-derived CDs causes damage to the cell surface, distortion to the cell wall as well as large-scale perturbation and cytoplasmic leakage in the periphery. Another study demonstrated that the exposure of microbial cells to CDs resulted in the collapse of the microbial cell membrane, lysis of intracellular materials, and deformation (Ezati, Rhim, Molaei, & Rezaei, 2022).

Cytotoxicity is one of the most important CDs features that must be considered in the biocompatibility of CDs for use in diagnostic and health risk analysis. CDs induce cytotoxicity through a combination of mechanisms including inflammation, oxidative stress, and autophagy (Huang & Tang, 2021). Here, the MTT colorimetric assay was used to assess the effect of the CDs on the viability of HCT-116 cells. In general, CDs synthesized from natural sources in a bottom-up approach have low toxicity and are non-toxic (Arul & Sethuraman, 2018; D. Wang et al., 2016). In this study, the viability of HCT-116 cells was not significantly altered by increasing CDs concentration to 5 mg/mL (Fig. 8 ). Similar effects were reported on 1.75 mg/mL postbiotics-derived CDs in HCT-116 and TM4 cells (Ghorbani et al., 2022), and 1 mg/mL for citric acid-derived CDs in normal (MCF-12A) and breast cancer (SKBR3) cell lines (Esfandiari et al., 2019). These data demonstrate that the current synthesized CDs can be considered potentially nontoxic nanomaterial (Huang & Tang, 2021). At 10 mg/mL, cellular viability significantly declined compared to the lower concentrations and the non-treated group. It should be noted that the cell morphology was modified (Fig. 8) in a concentration-dependent manner. There were no significant differences between the control group and the lowest concentration of CD (0.31 mg/mL), whereas at higher concentrations (e.g., 10 mg/mL), the number of viable cells decreased dramatically. Previous reports have indicated that CDs do not attack the nucleus but accumulate in the perinuclear area of live cells, and that no DNA damage can be expected in morphologically normal cells (Havrdová et al., 2021). When evaluating the toxicity of CDs, it is important to consider factors such as the characteristics and concentration of the CDs, the type of cell, and the duration of contact between the CDs and cells (Huang & Tang, 2021; Ashwani Kumar & Kumar, 2021) . In general, the cytotoxicity of CDs is governed by a mechanism dependent on their size. For example, smaller CDs (with a size of 2.2 nm) were found to be more toxic than larger CDs (with a size of 5.2 nm) in rat pheochromocytoma cell cultures (Hens et al., 2020). It is important to note that all the factors should be considered when comparing the results of different studies. However, Arul and Sethuraman (2018) synthesized biocompatible N-doped CDs (3.59 nm) from Actinidia deliciosa fruit extract with low toxicity at 5, 10, 15, 20, and 25 μL/mL in L929 cells. In a similar study, the authors reported that a concentration of 7 μL/mL had no effect, while at concentrations above ≥14 μL/mL, the synthesized CDs showed adverse effects on the cells (Zhang, Wang, Kong, Xue, & Tang, 2015). The cell type was emphasized as an important factor in a study by Havrdová et al. (2021), where the authors noted that the L929 cell line exhibited the highest CDs uptake within a certain concentration range compared to NIH/3T3 cells.

Sour whey solution was used to successfully synthesize semi-spherical P- and N-doped CDs less than 7.8 nm in size using a hydrothermal method. These CDs were found to possess moderate antimicrobial properties against some important foodborne pathogens and the ability to scavenge free radicals from the environment. The results of the antimicrobial tests and SEM observations of bacterial cells in the presence of CDs suggested that the cell wall may be the target of the antimicrobial mechanism of action of the synthesized CDs. In addition, CDs exhibited extremely low toxicity, making them promising candidates for use as UV-blockers and nanofillers in the development of composite polymers for food applications and as bioimaging agents. The utilization of whey byproducts as a CDs source not only reduces waste, but also provides a sustainable alternative to traditional methods that rely on non-renewable carbon sources. Finally, CDs show promise for food preservation due to their antimicrobial properties, stability, and low toxicity, but there are limitations: lack of long-term studies, limited spectrum of antimicrobial activity, the effect of particle size on CDs functionally, sensitivity to environmental conditions such as light, and lack of regulatory approval.

Mahmoud Esmaeili Koutamehr: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Mehran Moradi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Hossein Tajik: Supervision, Visualization. Rahim Molaei: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Maryam Khakbaz Heshmati: Investigation, manuscript editing, Writing – review & editing. Arash Alizadeh: Investigation, manuscript editing, Writing – review & editing.
